A stack overflow in Rust Binder found by syzbot was sent to me privately, and I'm trying to understand what's going on. The stack trace looks like this:

  • krealloc_noprof+0x8d/0x130 mm/slab_common.c:1288
  • <kernel::alloc::allocator::ReallocFunc>::call rust/kernel/alloc/allocator.rs:102 [inline]
  • <kernel::alloc::allocator::Kmalloc as kernel::alloc::Allocator>::realloc rust/kernel/alloc/allocator.rs:141 [inline]
  • <kernel::alloc::allocator::Kmalloc as kernel::alloc::Allocator>::alloc+0xb4/0x200 rust/kernel/alloc.rs:159
  • <KBox<ArcInner<core::mem::maybe_uninit::MaybeUninit<rust_binder_main::process::NodeRefInfo>>>>::new_uninit rust/kernel/alloc/kbox.rs:269 [inline]
  • <KBox<ArcInner<MaybeUninit<rust_binder_main::process::NodeRefInfo>>> as InPlaceInit<_>>::try_init::<AllocError, _> rust/kernel/alloc/kbox.rs:386 [inline]
  • <UniqueArc<rust_binder_main::process::NodeRefInfo>>::new_uninit rust/kernel/sync/arc.rs:646 [inline]
  • <rust_binder_main::process::Process>::insert_or_update_handle+0x3ed/0x1e70 drivers/android/binder/process.rs:845
  • <rust_binder_main::allocation::AllocationView>::transfer_binder_object+0x1d1/0x6b0 drivers/android/binder/allocation.rs:398
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::translate_object+0xeeb/0x6640 drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:793
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::copy_transaction_data+0x300b/0x57b0 drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:1186
  • <rust_binder_main::transaction::Transaction>::new+0x3a2/0x2150 drivers/android/binder/transaction.rs:81
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::transaction_inner drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:1350 [inline]
  • <<rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::transaction_inner as core::ops::function::FnOnce<(&Arc<rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>, &rust_binder_main::defs::BinderTransactionDataSg)>>::call_once usr/local/rustup/toolchains/1.91.1-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/lib/rustlib/src/rust/library/core/src/ops/function.rs:250 [inline]
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::transaction::<<rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::transaction_inner>+0x8c4/0x1110 drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:1325
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::write+0x1277/0xa690 drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:1456
  • <rust_binder_main::thread::Thread>::write_read drivers/android/binder/thread.rs:1612 [inline]
  • <rust_binder_main::process::Process>::ioctl_write_read drivers/android/binder/process.rs:1609 [inline]
  • <rust_binder_main::process::Process>::ioctl drivers/android/binder/process.rs:1674 [inline]
  • rust_binder_main::rust_binder_ioctl+0x1077/0x5da0 drivers/android/binder/rust_binder_main.rs:449
  • vfs_ioctl fs/ioctl.c:51 [inline]
  • __do_sys_ioctl fs/ioctl.c:907 [inline]
  • __se_sys_ioctl+0x135/0x1b0 fs/ioctl.c:893
  • __x64_sys_ioctl+0x7f/0xa0 fs/ioctl.c:893
  • x64_sys_call+0x1878/0x2ee0 arch/x86/include/generated/asm/syscalls_64.h:17
  • do_syscall_x64 arch/x86/entry/common.c:47 [inline]
  • do_syscall_64+0x57/0xf0 arch/x86/entry/common.c:78
  • entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x76/0x7e

And then inside krealloc it goes into some crazy deep callstack including kasan_save_stack and eventually hits the guard page.

How can I tell whether this is just KASAN doing crazy stuff, or actually my stack is too deep. None of the Rust Binder functions exceed the CONFIG_FRAME_WARN limit with KASAN off, though they are still rather large:

  • ioctl_write_read is 856 bytes
  • copy_transaction_data 504 bytes
  • Transaction::new is 586 bytes

Am I using too much stack or is it just KASAN?

Compudj@irc.oftc.net: Hi ! boqun told me you were interested in hazard pointers
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: I did the recent hazard pointer API prototype at https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260223204418.1429025-1-mathieu.desnoyers@efficios.com/
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: I'd be interested to discuss worker thread requirements
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: one thing I see is distinct between RCU and HP is that HP are inherently specific to pointers
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: so you end up synchronizing against a pointer, set of pointers, or range of addresses, rather than read-side critical sections
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: one thing I've been considering a good fit for HP is to work on "sets" of pointers to synchronize against
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: which can be optimized with bloom filters
@Gary Guo @Alvin Sun ^

Compudj@irc.oftc.net: a worker thread could keep two unordered sets of pointers. One being the currently waited for, and the other being the set of new requests, into which we could also move away the pointers we would need to busy wait on
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: each "pointer" wait request would have a wait queue, and could be used with a polling interface
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: I just wonder if that thread should be handling its work periodically, or be asleep waiting for a wakeup
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: if it's a wakeup scheme, then we'd need to issue the wakeup from the hazptr release slowpath

Compudj@irc.oftc.net: AFAIU, what we could do is: for the preallocated percpu slots, make the worker thread busy wait (those are short lived anyway). For the overflow list, those would wakeup the worker thread when there are updates.

I was just following Gary's suggestion, using his LazyRevocable implementation (which originally uses RCU internally, which cannot sleep) combined with your hazard pointer to implement debugfs data sharing for the Tyr module and driver.

The hazptr_synchronize is only called during module unloading, so the busy-waiting overhead should be acceptable in this simple scenario. I'm still somewhat new to hazard pointers, but if you need any help with verification, I'd be happy to assist.

for the preallocated percpu slots, make the worker thread busy wait (those are short lived anyway)

I'm not sure if this is will be true in general. If we implement sleepable revocable using hazptr, then the reader side might go into sleep which won't be short-lived?

My prior experience with hazard ptr is via the Rust arc-swap crate, and the use cases there is definitely not always short-lived. You might be holding on to a slot for very long time.

However, arc-swap is very difficult way of using hazard ptr compared to the current hazard ptr series. It combines reference counting with hazard ptr, so if synchronize side finds out the pointer is being used, it just increases the reference count on behalf of the reader side, and when the reader side release the slot it will figure this out and it would need to drop the reference count.
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: it moves the HP from preallocated slots to overflow list on preemption
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: my hazard pointer implementation has a scheduler hook
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: so technically anything that sleeps is magically moved to the overflow list
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: this means the synchronize hp can busy-wait on anything that dits in the preallocated percpu slots, and use a wait/wakeup scheme for overflow list
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: dits->sits
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: this also means that the hp release fast-path won't have to issue a wakeup
Muvinus@irc.oftc.net: Hoooooral
Muvinus@irc.oftc.net: Hoorah Rust
Compudj@irc.oftc.net: for those who have not seen it, I've posted a RFC about my new RCU Trie: https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/b7b20a93-0310-48c1-90b7-6c12fafc784e@efficios.com/
